title: Sticky toffee pudding cake
subtitle: Date sponge with toffee sauce, baked as a traybake
excerpt: A traybake version of the British classic: a dark, date-filled sponge soaked while warm with a rich toffee sauce. Served in squares with more sauce and double cream.
sourceNotes: Sticky toffee pudding was created at the Sharrow Bay Hotel, Ullswater, in the 1970s, attributed to Francis Coulson. The date sponge and toffee sauce formula is well documented. This traybake version follows the same composition but scales for a 20×30 cm tin.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the sponge before adding the sauce. Add warm sauce after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge keeps for 3 days. Reheat squares in a microwave with a spoonful of sauce.

glossaryTerms.definition: A sauce made by melting butter with brown sugar, then adding cream and simmering until thickened. The heat caramelises the sugars and the cream emulsifies into the butter, producing a pourable, glossy sauce. Poured over warm sponge it soaks into the surface; served cold it sets thicker.

content.text: The date sponge is dark, dense, and moist from the cooked dates folded through it. The sauce is what makes it: a generous amount poured over the sponge while both are still warm, so it soaks into the top and forms a sticky, glossy layer. Do not be conservative with the sauce.

Put the {{dried-dates}} in a small saucepan with 175 ml of water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 4 minutes until very soft. Remove from the heat, stir in the {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} (it will foam briefly) and leave to cool for 10 minutes.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 20×30 cm tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} (for the sponge) and {{light-brown-sugar}} together until pale. Beat in the {{free-range-eggs}} and {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}}. Stir in the cooked date mixture. Pour into the tin.
Bake for 25–30 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. While it bakes, make the sauce.
Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} (for the sauce) and {{dark-brown-sugar}} in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring. Add the {{double-cream}} and {{fine-sea-salt}}. Simmer for 3–4 minutes until the
is glossy and coats a spoon.
As soon as the sponge comes out of the oven, pour two-thirds of the sauce over the top and leave for 10 minutes to soak in. Cut into squares and serve with the remaining sauce alongside.
